_schema_note: Ground truth bugs planted in eval/code_fixture.ts for grounded-review code-mode eval. 5 defects across null/sql/logic/type/silent categories. Matching is keyword-substring on reviewer finding text (case-insensitive). If ANY keyword in keywords_any appears in a reviewer finding, that finding is attributed to this B#.

findings.description: The loop `for (let i = 0; i < invoices.length - 1; i++)` skips the last invoice. Should be `i < invoices.length` (or `i <= invoices.length - 1`). The -1 is wrong. Result: monthly total is underreported by one invoice per user, silent data corruption.

Line 37 dereferences `user.profile.name` without checking if `user` is null or `profile` is undefined. findUser (line 23-29) explicitly returns `User | null`, and the User interface marks profile as optional (`profile?: ...`). Calling monthlyTotal with an id for a deleted user will throw `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'profile')`.

Line 56 uses loose equality `discountFlag == "0"`. The parameter type is `string | number`, so passing the number `0` will coerce to string `"0"` and match, which may or may not be intended. More critically, passing the string `"00"` does NOT match but represents a semantic zero. The right fix is either strict equality + explicit type handling, or normalize the input before comparing.

Line 41 builds the SQL query with a template literal that interpolates `userId` directly: `` `SELECT ... WHERE user_id = '${userId}'` ``. This is a classic SQL injection vector: a userId like `x' OR '1'='1` would unlock all invoices. findUser on line 27 correctly uses parameterized queries (`?` placeholder); monthlyTotal should do the same: `db.query('SELECT ... WHERE user_id = ?', [userId])`.

Line 67 has a bare `catch {}` that discards the caught exception and returns `false`. The caller sees a boolean and cannot distinguish 'email service down' from 'invalid email address' from 'network timeout'. Production debugging becomes impossible because the original error is dropped with no log. Minimum fix: log the error with `console.error(err)` before returning false, or re-throw after logging.
